tldr: Inference notebook that loads a DINOv2-based segmenter and a custom CNN, applies test-time augmentation and ensemble averaging, applies dynamic thresholding and morphological filtering, and generates a Kaggle submission with RLE masks or an 'authentic' fallback.
summary: This notebook demonstrates a complete inference and submission pipeline for an image forgery detection competition. It loads two pre-trained models (a DINOv2-based segmenter and a lightweight CNN encoder-decoder), processes test images at 512x512 resolution, and applies test-time augmentation before averaging predictions. Post-processing includes Gaussian blurring, dynamic thresholding, morphological operations, and area/confidence filtering to decide between generating an RLE mask or submitting 'authentic'. The author explains the RLE encoding format and justifies the 'authentic-only' baseline strategy for highly imbalanced datasets.
extensive_summary:
## Understanding RLE & Submission Format
The notebook begins by explaining the Run-Length Encoding (RLE) metric used for segmentation submissions. It provides a custom `rle_encode` function that flattens a binary mask, pads it with zeros, finds transition points, calculates run lengths, and formats them as a space-separated string. For empty masks, it returns the string "authentic". Visualization helpers display masks with pixel values and grid lines.

## Model Architecture & Loading
The inference pipeline loads two models from Kaggle datasets. The first is a `DinoSegmenter` that uses `transformers.AutoModel` (DINOv2) as a frozen encoder. It extracts the `last_hidden_state`, discards the CLS token (`[:, 1:, :]`), reshapes the feature map to `(B, C, s, s)`, and passes it through a custom `Decoder` (Conv2d 768→256→64→1 with ReLU activations) to predict per-pixel logits. The second model is a lightweight CNN `Model` with a 5-layer encoder (Conv-ReLU-MaxPool blocks) and a 4-layer decoder (Conv-Upsample blocks), outputting a single-channel mask. A robust `load_model` function handles various checkpoint formats (state_dict, model object, etc.) and moves weights to CUDA.

## Inference & Test-Time Augmentation (TTA)
Test images are resized to 512x512 and normalized to [0, 1]. For each image, predictions are generated using `predict_with_tta`, which applies four augmentations: original, horizontal flip, vertical flip, and 90-degree rotation (with corresponding inverse rotations applied to the output masks). Predictions are sigmoid-activated and stacked. The two models' TTA predictions are averaged. 

## Post-Processing & Submission
The averaged prediction undergoes Gaussian blurring (3x3 kernel), followed by dynamic thresholding: `thr = mean(pred) + 0.3 * std(pred)`. The binary mask is cleaned with morphological closing (5x5) and opening (3x3), then resized to the original image dimensions using nearest-neighbor interpolation. If the mask area is below 150 pixels or the mean confidence inside the mask is below 0.33, the annotation defaults to "authentic". Otherwise, the mask is re-encoded via the custom RLE function. Results are merged with the sample submission CSV and saved.
preprocessing: DINOv2 processor converts images to byte tensors
ensemble: Averages sigmoid-activated predictions from a DINOv2-based segmenter and a custom CNN encoder-decoder.
post_processing: Gaussian blur (3x3), dynamic thresholding (mean + 0.3 * std), morphological closing (5x5) and opening (3x3), resize to original resolution with nearest-neighbor interpolation, area filter (>150) and confidence filter (>0.33) to fallback to 'authentic'
notable_techniques: Extracting DINOv2 feature maps by discarding the CLS token and reshaping `last_hidden_state` to spatial dimensions
Dynamic thresholding using `mean + 0.3 * std` instead of fixed values
Fallback to 'authentic' submission when predicted mask area or confidence falls below thresholds
Robust checkpoint loading that handles nested state dicts and model objects
